text: Hey everyone, I'm Shaw. This is the third video in a larger series on reinforcement learning with LLMs. Here I'll walk through a concrete example of using RL to fine-tune GPTO4 mini. Reinforcement learning allows LLMs to come up with better responses through trial and error. In the last video of this series, we discussed three ways people are doing this today. The first was through so-called reinforcement learning from human feedback. With this approach, an LLM will generate responses to requests and get feedback on whether a human likes that response or not. And over time, the LLM's responses become more and more aligned with human preferences. While RHF is a powerful technique, it relies on human annotators to come up with labeled preference data to enable this training loop, which introduces bottlenecks and big resource requirements. One way to scale up this process at a lower cost is through so-called reinforcement learning from AI feedback, which basically replaces the human annotator with another LLM. However, the latest way people are using RL to train LLMs is fundamentally different, and that's through reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards. Instead of scoring an LLM's response based on human preference data or AI feedback, here the feedback is based on a verifiable reward. So this will be some kind of codebased check of the model's response to see if it satisfies a predefined rubric. Put another way, RLVR doesn't just train the model to generate good responses, but trains the model to achieve outcomes. So just to recap this process from last time, the LLM will receive a request that has a ground truth or correct answer. Then the LLM will generate a response to this request. And then this response will be evaluated just using code. And so this strategy of training LLMs on tasks with verifiable right answers is the key technique behind modern AI agents such as deep research tools or coding agents and many other domain specific agents that we're seeing these days. And so just to give an example of the benefits of RLVR versus supervised fine-tuning, suppose we wanted to train an AI agent to understand which emails that hit our inbox are important versus not important. If we wanted to do this using supervised fine-tuning, what this would consist of is we would write some kind of system prompt and then gather a training data set of emails along with labels of important or not important. And we'd basically teach the LLM how to do this task by example. So we would show it hundreds or thousands or even more examples of emails that have hit our inbox and have a ground truth classification for it that the model can learn. However, there's a key problem here with this setup. We're expecting the model to look at the email and then immediately come up with a classification. So this significantly limits the amount of computation the LLM can use to do this classification task. However, with reinforcement fine-tuning, we can prepare the same exact data set. So, we have a bunch of emails from our inbox and its corresponding classification. However, now we're not limiting the amount of compute the LLM can use to perform this task. Because with reinforcement fine-tuning, the LLM has the freedom to think through the problem before coming up with its final classification. This is powerful because the LLM can potentially have this adaptive reasoning process. So for emails that are pretty easy to classify, it won't generate a lot of tokens, but for emails that are pretty hard to classify, it can really analyze the email and come up with a final classification. So to make this all more concrete, I'm going to walk through a specific example of training GPT04 mini to perform a text classification task using RLVR. Here I've got a HDFS data set. You can imagine a company or organization has some large scale data infrastructure that's running on Hadoop and constantly the system is spitting out some steady stream of logs. Some of the logs will just be business as usual, but other logs might be indicating something is going wrong. So, it'd be great if we could have some system that's constantly monitoring these logs and then alerting the DevOps team anytime the log spits out some kind of anomaly, whether it's some kind of hacking attempt or just something's going wrong with the system. So here we've got that same picture of RLVR, but in this case the requests will specifically be these HDFS log blocks. HDFS stands for Hadoop distributed file system. So just this massive distributed system where you can store immense volumes of data. We're going to get blocks. So this could be multiple lines from the logs and pass it to an LLM. And we're going to have the LLM classify these blocks as normal or anomalous and then give us a explanation of why it made its classification. And then finally, we can just run some code and compare the LLM's classification with the ground truth. So this is going to be the training loop. And for this, we're going to be using OpenAI's reinforcement fine-tuning API. The data I'm using is freely available on hugging face. and everything's coded in Python. This is going to be a five-step process to do the training. So, the first thing we're going to do is create a train validation split. So, we'll have some number of examples for training the model and some other set of examples to monitor it during training. Next, we're going to format the data. So, OpenAI's reinforcement fine-tuning API expects the data in a particular format. So I'll need to transform it from CSV like into this JSONL format. Then we'll create a greater. So this is just going to be that codebased check of the LLM's output versus the ground truth. Then we'll kick off the training run using OpenAI's API. And then finally, we'll compare the baseline model to the fine-tuned model. Before we do anything, we'll need to import some libraries. First, I'll load this data set from the hugging face hub. We can just do that with one line. And the data set looks like this. So, there are three columns. There's a unique ID for each block of text. There's going to be a text column. So, this is the content of each block. And then we have the ground truth label. So, zero means that the block is normal. and then one means the block is anomalous. So the contents of the block just looks like a readout from the terminal. So you have some process running and it's spitting out logs constantly. This is one specific example and this one has a label of normal. The data set here has on the order of 100,000 examples, but you can get the link to the full data set in the description below. Now that we have the data set loaded, now we can create the split. So I'm going to just have a 100 examples for training and then 50 examples for validation. One of the challenges with this data set is that most blocks are not anomalous. So maybe there's an anomaly like 2% of the time. This introduces a problem when we're trying to train a model on these examples. Because if we just have a 100 training examples and only 2% of them are anomalies, that doesn't give the model a lot of opportunity for learning. it doesn't give it a whole lot of potential training signals. So here I'm going to resample the training data to just give the model more opportunities to actually learn something. Here I'm splitting the training data set and the validation data set into anomalous and normal subsets. So this will just be a data set of all the anomalous examples in the training data. This will be all the normal examples in the training data. This will be all the anomalous examples in the development or validation data. And then this will be all the normal examples in the validation data set. And then from here we can just randomly sample 100 examples for the training and 50 examples for the validation set. So at the end of this we'll have 100 training examples. Half of them are anomalous, half of them are normal. And then for validation we have 10 anomalous and 40 normal. The reason I went for 8020 at the validation is again if we have only 2% as anomalous even if we're not training the model on this data we're still trying to evaluate the model on this data. So if most of them are normal there's not much potential signal in the data set but at the same time we don't want it to just be 50/50 because in the real world the data distribution is skewed. So here I just went with 8020 as like somewhere in the middle of lots of evaluation signal and the natural distribution of these anomalous blocks. Now that we've got the training and validation data sets, now we need to format the data in a way that is compatible with OpenAI's reinforcement fine-tuning API. So here I've got this little helper function which will take in an example. So it'll take in a row from the data set we saw on hugging face and it'll put it into the right JSON L format. So this is expecting basically a dictionary with a messages key value pair and a label key value pair. The messages will just be like the developer message and the user message. The developer message has our system prompt. So classify this HDFS log block as anomalous or normal. And then we have the user message which is just the HDFS log block. Finally, the label is just the ground truth. So this will be true or false. So true means anomalous, false means normal. And then we can just apply this helper function to our training data in our validation data like this. This will go through each example in the training sample and create a list of dictionaries in the proper format. This is what one example will look like. We've got the developer message and then we've got the user message and then we just got this big block of text for the LLM to classify and then we have the ground truth. So false means that this is a normal block. As a final step, we'll need to take this list of dictionaries that we've created in Python and export it as this JSONL file format. Here I'm just defining the paths to save the data at. And then here we're just going to write the files. We're opening the file and then we'll go through the training data. So train data is again a list of dictionaries. So we'll go through one item at a time through this list and then write it to this JSON L file. So each line is in the JSON format and we we'll write it one line at a time for the training data and the validation data. Finally, we can just upload both these files to OpenAI's API. Now we've got our training data and our validation data uploaded to OpenAI. Next thing we need to do is to create a so-called grader. So, this is going to just be some software that compares the LLM's output to the ground truth label. Here, I'm going to create a custom Python grader. The way we define this is a little wonky because we're creating this JSON thing that we're going to send with a API request. But just to walk through this, we can have different types of graders. So here I've got a Python grader which just means that we can use arbitrary Python code to do the evaluation. We can give our grader a name. So I just called it custom reward. The image tag, I'm not sure what this is, but it was required. So I just did whatever was in the docs. And then the source is the actual code for this grader. So the way this going to work, it's going to take in a sample. So this is going to be the generated output of the LLM. And then it's going to also take in an item. And so this is going to be one of the training examples that we uploaded to OpenAI's API. And then it's just going to run through this code here. First, it's going to extract the LLM's prediction. And so the prediction is going to be in a structured output. So it's going to extract this anomalous value. And then it's going to get the actual label. It's going to do this by extracting the label value from the item. And then it's just going to compare these two things together. and produce a reward for four different situations. If the model gets the prediction right, that'll be a correct prediction. It's going to return a one. However, if the model does a false negative, so it misses an anomaly, we'll return zero. And then finally, if the model gets a false positive, so if it marks something as an anomaly, which is not an anomaly, we'll give it a slightly higher reward. So basically what we've codified here is one we want the model to make correct predictions but also we'd rather the model be safe as opposed to sorry. So it's okay if it's a little conservative with its predictions but we want to give more weight to spotting an anomaly than missing one. Finally, this is for the case where the LLM didn't generate a valid structured output. So we'll just give it a zero reward for this case as well. So with the greater we were actually extracting the prediction from the LLM's output. And the way we're doing that is by using structured outputs. There's a lot of metadata here, but the two things to look at is that we're going to force the model to have a structured output with two attributes. The first attribute is anomalous, which is basically a boolean. It's a true or false if the log block is anomalous or not. and two is going to be an explanation of why the LLM made the classification that it did. So this will just be a string and it'll be an explanation of the model's decision. The reason we have to do this is we actually don't have access to the reasoning trace that the model generates just because OpenAI does not make that available. This allows us to have some sense of why the model made the prediction that it did. Now that we've got our data, we've created a greater, and we've created the structured output format for the LLM, we can now fine-tune it. So, right now, as of the recording of this video, the only model available in OpenAI's reinforcement fine-tuning API is 04 mini 20250416. So, that's what I'm using here. To create the fine-tuning job, we can do it like this. We'll pass in our base model. We can create a suffix to help us identify this fine-tuning job. We'll specify the training file. And so this is the file we just uploaded to the API. We can specify a validation file. This is the validation file we uploaded. And then we're going to define the training details and hyperparameters. We're going to use reinforcement fine-tuning. And then here we're going to input our greater and our response format for the structured outputs. We could also specify hyperparameters here, but I went ahead and omitted them and allowed the API to just come up with these automatically and they look like this. So, it's going to train for one epoch. So, that means it's going to just go through all of the training data. They're going to be a batch size of five. So, each training step will consist of five examples. We have 100 training examples and we'll go through five at a time. So, there will be 20 training steps. The learning rate multiplier just sets how fast the model's weights will be updated. Eval interval defines the number of steps in between running the validation data through the model. And then everything else we just set as the default. And then here we can also see how long it took to run this training job. So this took 2 hours and 22 minutes. So it did take quite a long time even though this isn't a massive data set. And that's just because reinforcement learning is a very computationally expensive way of training models. Additionally, OpenAI doesn't give a whole lot of details on what's going on under the hood. So, it's not clear whether they're doing PO style or gRPO style fine-tuning, but I guess by not providing those details, the user interface is a bit simpler. This is just a plot of the training run. So, the green is the training data set. The purple is the validation data set. We can see the validation data has results for every five steps. And so it kind of like is a little windy, but there's a little bit of improvement with the validation set. The training set starts pretty low, has a 0.55 reward, and then it kind of winds up and down and then kind of crashes at the end. But hopefully this last one is the one we went with. And we can also view the reasoning tokens over time. So we can see the longer the model is trained for, the more it thinks through the problem. And then on the 20th step, it kind of went crazy where we went from a mean of around 600 reasoning tokens to 1,600. So like a 3x increase. And that also corresponded with a drop in the training reward. So it seems like the 20th step wasn't a very good one. This is just giving you a flavor of the volatility of reinforcement learning. And this is why just using a 100 examples here is not ideal because one way we can mitigate this volatility is by allowing the model to train for longer on more examples and hopefully over time all this noise will cancel out. But we can look at the final results by comparing the baseline model to this fine-tuned model to see if this actually helped us in this HDFS classification task. So to do this, I'm going to create a testing sample. This is going to be done in the same exact way I created the training and the validation splits. So I'm going to randomly sample anomalous and normal bits from testing. And then here I'm going to do a 9010 split. Here again I'm resampling the data because if we have 50 examples and only one of them is anomalous, we can't really get much of a evaluation signal out of that. However, at the same time, the data set is imbalanced and we want to evaluate the model's performance on the real world natural distribution. So here I just went with 9010 as something in the middle of optimal evaluation signal and something close to the natural distribution. I end up with five anomalous examples and 45 normal examples. So another solution would be instead of just 50 examples, I could look at like 5,000 examples with the natural distribution. But then that would be very expensive because we have to pay for these API calls. Just to process 50 of them takes 6 minutes. So if we did 100 times more, it would take like 600 minutes, which would take about 10 hours to process. But here are the results. The first row is the baseline model. So it had about 80% accuracy, but that's not very helpful here because 90% of the examples are normal. So even if the model just guessed normal for all the log blocks, it would get 90% accuracy. So that's why it's important to also look at precision, recall, and F1 score. And by all accounts, the fine-tuned model outperformed the base model. So not only was it more accurate, but it had better precision, it had better recall, and it had a better F1 score. And so the main thing I look at when evaluating imbalance data is the F1 score, which is basically a mix of the precision and recall into a single metric. We can see that the F1 score increased by 0.4, which is a significant increase. Another thing we can look at here are so-called confusion matrices. This is showing us the number of true negatives, true positives, false positives, and false negatives. We see for the base model that it had 37 true negatives, two true positives. So it got two out of the five anomalous predictions, right? But it missed three of them. So that's what we're seeing here. It predicted a zero three times when it was actually a one. And then eight times it predicted a one when it was actually a zero. But for the fine-tune model, it performed much better. So a handful more true negatives, doubled the amount of true positives. So it just missed one of the anomalies. and then it had three false positives. So three times it marked something as an anomaly when it really wasn't one. So really this bottom left block is the problem. This is when you classify something as normal that was an anomaly. That's much more harmful than predicting something as an anomaly when it's actually normal. Cuz in the context of this system, this classification might just be used to alert a DevOps engineer to take a closer look at that specific log. Sure, it's not ideal to have a engineer look at something that is normal, especially if you're paying them like six figures or something, but it's much worse to not alert a DevOps engineer and miss some kind of large-scale failure of the file system or some kind of hacking attempt from a bad actor. From this, reinforcement learning did a good job of improving the model's performance on this specific task. However, there were definitely some limitations to this process. First and foremost, RL is expensive. While the supervised fine-tuning APIs aren't too bad, the reinforcement fine-tuning API costs $100 an hour for training. So, for training this model over 2 hours and 22 minutes, I paid $250. Paying this once isn't a big deal, especially if you have the right use case for it. But the problem is because reinforcement learning can be so volatile and can be so unstable. Really, you don't want to just run one training run, you're going to want to run a handful. I would have liked to experiment with different mixes of training data. So here I did 50/50 for training, but what about 8020 or 9010 or trying to do the natural distribution but then just 10xing or 100xing the training data size. But of course, if I 100xed the training data size, this price could have 100xed as well. So instead of just $250, it would have cost $25,000. And then if I'm running multiple experiments of that, it's going to get quite expensive. This just introduces some limitations on how freely and how quickly you can iterate and experiment with reinforcement learning as opposed to supervised fine-tuning. The second thing was the imbalanced data. Trying to find this balance between training signal and the real world distribution of anomalies. If you stick to the natural distribution and you only have 100 training examples, there are only two anomalies in that data set. So there's very weak signal for the model to learn from and it might not learn anything after that 100 samples. On the flip side, by doing the 50/50 training, there's a risk that the model learns to overestimate the number of anomalies in the real world. While that didn't seem to be the case with that data set here, that is always something to keep in mind when working with imbalanced data. Finally, a big limitation of using an OpenAI reasoning model for this is that they don't give you access to the reasoning traces. So, we kind of worked around that by adding that reasoning explanation to the output, but ideally we would be able to see the full reasoning trace and be able to read through the model's logic for each of its classifications. then that could help us inform better system prompts or better training examples to ultimately improve the model's performance. That brings us to what's next. One way we can mitigate some of these problems is instead of using OpenAI's API using open- source solutions. So using things from the hugging face ecosystem like TRL or using Unsloth which allows for the efficient fine-tuning of models. The two benefits of open source is that since we have full control over the training process, we can make it as efficient as possible and hopefully get the training cost and the compute cost as low as possible to hopefully run on just like a laptop or something. And two, because we are using open- source models, we'll have access to the full reasoning trace and can have a good understanding of why the model makes the classification that it does. So, if you have any questions about anything I talked about here or suggestions for future videos in this series, please let me know in the comments below.
